titleStmt.title: The Ambassador in Germany ( (Dodd) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Ambassador in Germany ( Dodd ) to the Secretary of State
div.p:
75. So far as could be ascertained from the Foreign Office and otherwise, the only local development yesterday, which doubtless is more fully covered from London, is the pressure being put on Germany to make some gesture to France in withdrawing part of the Rhineland zone forces in order to help the British in their discussions in London. Apparently from long conversations with the French Embassy here this is a matter of real importance and possibly could be the turning point in the situation. When I discussed this with Dieckhoff the impression was gained that the Germans intended to stand pat for the time being following Hoesch’s reply to Eden of Thursday.March 12; see declaration by the German Government, Documents on International Affairs, 1936 (London, Oxford University Press, 1937), p. 81.
This for the reason that the Germans do not feel that the critical point in the present stage of the discussions in London will be reached for some time and also because they do not believe that it can result in worse than an impasse. It may be that the Germans are holding in reserve the idea set forth in my 69, March 11, 1 p.m. Regarding representation, Dieckhoff said that they had received nothing further than the formal League notifications; that they were not sending anyone at present and would consider the question if and when they were expressly invited.

In addition to the many rumors and conjectures prevalent at this stage of such a situation we found that a number of the most experienced observers were thinking along the lines of our 74, March 12, 8 p.m., considering that the psychological features are perhaps the most important to estimate in so far as practicable. Likewise the question was frequently raised as to just what position the Army would take in the final development of the situation. We found complete agreement in accord with our own ideas that the Army and people would be united behind Hitler if there were an invasion of the Reich or even an ultimatum without a face saving clause. There was disagreement as to what the Army might do if the League or Locarno Powers showed a united front and made some stiff demands constituting a climb down by Germany in the Rhineland which would not be too drastic and showing a desire to discuss the constructive features of Hitler’s memorandum of last Saturday.
Repeated Paris, London, Rome, and airmail to Brussels, Geneva.
